Another example is the Aaland Islands, where after an investigation the League managed to settle a dispute between Sweden and Finland. The League was also successful in improving living standards. For example they provided direct aid and welfare to people in need around the world. One of their famous acts is the repatriation of 400,000 WWI prisoners, whom they employed them to kill mosquitos to prevent malaria and also to prevent leprosy. [Since you are talking about grammar in general, there is no need for "the" to precede it. However, if you are talking about any singular grammar, then you should precede with "the". Something is redundant here. Derived is better than summarized because it conveys that common usage was instrumental in the formation of grammar. You can either use "a" and change "numbers" to singular "number" as in "a large number of" or just get rid of "a" as in "large numbers of". The phrase "large numbers of language usage instances that were really used by a lot of people in daily life" is long and unwieldy, it is better to make use of implied language context and say something like "popular usage", this is a large reduction in words so you may also want to retain your elaboration so altogether, two possible suggested sentences could be "Grammar is derived from popular usage." or "The grammar, of any particular language, is derived from popular usage by its speakers."
